coredata.dc:title: VEGF mimetic peptide-conjugated nanoparticles for magnetic resonance imaging and therapy of myocardial infarction
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Bing
Li, Yingxu
Chen, Shuangling
Wang, Yuji
Zheng, Yuanyuan
coredata.dc:description: To reduce the mortality of myocardial infarction (MI), accurate detection of the infarct and appropriate prevention against ischemia/reperfusion (I/R) induced cardiac dysfunction are highly desired. Considering that vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) receptors are overexpressed in the infarcted heart and VEGF mimetic peptide QK binds specifically to VEGF receptors and activates vascularization, the PEG-QK-modified, gadolinium-doped carbon dots (GCD-PEG-QK) were formulated. This research aims to investigate the magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) capability of GCD-PEG-QK on myocardial infarct and their therapeutic effect on I/R-induced myocardial injury. These multifunctional nanoparticles exhibited good colloidal stability, excellent fluorescent and magnetic property, and satisfactory biocompatibility. Intravenous injection of GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles post myocardial I/R displayed accurate MRI of the infarct, enhanced efficacy of QK peptide on pro-angiogenesis, and amelioration of cardiac fibrosis, remodeling and dysfunction, probably via the improvement on QK's in vivo stability and MI-targeting. Collectively, the data suggested that this theranostic nanomedicine can realize precise MRI and effective therapy for acute MI in a non-invasive manner.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GCD-SH nanoparticles. (A) TEM morphology of GCD-SH nanoparticles (scale bar =20 nm). Insets showed the pictures of GCD-SH dispersed in distilled water, PBS (pH 7.4), and DMEM medium (containing 10% FBS). (B) Hydrodynamic diameter distribution of GCD-SH. (C) Zeta potentials of GCD-SH dispersed in distilled water, PBS (pH 7.4), and DMEM medium (containing 10% FBS) from day 1–14. (D) UV − vis absorbance and photoluminescence spectra of GCD-SH. Inset is the photograph of GCD-SH dispersed in water under UV lamp. (E) Photoluminescence spectra of GCD-SH with various excitation wavelengths. (F) The fluorescence intensity of GCD-SH at various pH values.

Characterization of GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles. (A) TEM morphology of GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles (scale bar =50 nm). Inset is the enlarged photo in the box and the pictures of GCD-PEG-QK dispersed in distilled water, PBS (pH 7.4), and DMEM medium (containing 10% FBS). (B) Hydrodynamic diameter distribution of GCD-PEG-QK. (C) CD spectra of GCD-PEG, QK and GCD-PEG-QK. (D) UV − vis absorbance of GCD-SH, GCD-PEG, QK, and GCD-PEG-QK. (E) FT-IR spectra of GCD-SH, PEG, QK and (F) GCD-PEG-QK.

Cardiac MR imaging of I/R mice by GCD-SH or GCD-PEG-QK. (A) Representative MR imaging of the infarcted myocardium post-i.v. injection of GCD-SH or (B) GCD-PEG-QK. Yellow circles showed the hyperenhancement indicative of the infarct. (C) CNR of the infarcted versus non-infarcted myocardium during the first 24 h post-i.v. injection of GCD-PEG-QK or GCD-SH. Data are expressed as means ± SD, n = 3. ⁎⁎P˂ 0.01 GCD-PEG-QK vs. GCD-SH. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cardiac biodistribution of nanoparticles and VEGFR (flt1 and Kdr). (A) Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of the infarcted and remote regions at 4 and 24 h post-i.v. injection of GCD-PEG-QK or (B) GCD-PEG nanoparticles. Row 1: nanoparticles (blue); row 2: Flt1 (red); row 3: merge of row 1 and 2; row 4: nanoparticles (blue); row 5: Kdr (green); row 6: merge of row 4 and 5. n = 4. Scale bar: 100 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Effects of GCD-PEG-QK on angiogenesis of HUVECs. (A) The wound-healing migration assay with the treatment of vehicle, GCD-PEG, GCD-PEG-QK or QK. Scale bars, 100 μm. (B) Tube formation of HUVECs on Matrigel incubated with vehicle, GCD-PEG, GCD-PEG-QK or QK under H/R condition. Scale bars, 200 μm. (C) The percentage of wound closed at 12 and 24 h after administration in each group. (D) Quantitative results showing the number of branches in tube formation assay. Data are expressed as means ± SD, n = 6. ## P < 0.01 vs. Control. ⁎⁎ P < 0.01 vs. H/R group.

Heart function of mice with the administration of saline, QK or GCD-PEG-QK. (A) Schematic showing the animal study design. (B) ECG representations before and after coronary artery ligation. (C) Echocardiographic representations of mice at day-1, 14, and 28 post myocardial I/R surgery. (D) The LVEF%, (E) diastolic (LVAW; d) and (F) systolic LV anterior wall (LVAW; s) at day 1–28 post myocardial I/R surgery in each group. (G) The differences of LVEF (ΔLVEF 14–1 day) between day-14 and -1, and between day-28 and -1 (ΔLVEF 28–1 day) after I/R in each group. Data are expressed as means ± SD, n = 6–8. & P < 0.05, && P < 0.01 vs. Sham; ⁎ P < 0.05, ⁎⁎ P < 0.01 vs. I/R + Saline.

GCD-PEG-QK administration promoted angiogenesis and ameliorated cell apoptosis of I/R heart. (A) Immunofluorescence staining of α-SMA and (B) Pecam1 in the border zone of the infarcted heart or normal heart at 14 days after myocardial I/R surgery. Scale bar, 100 μm. (C) Statistical analysis of α-SMA+ round vessels and (D) Pecam1+ cells from 12 randomly chosen fields of four mice in each group. (E) The relative mRNA level of Pecam1 (normalized by GAPDH) detected at 14 days after myocardial I/R surgery. (F) Representative and magnified view of TUNEL staining in the border zone of heart at 14 days after myocardial I/R. (G) The statistical analysis of TUNEL staining from 12 randomly chosen fields of four mice in each group. (H) The caspase-3 activity of heart in each group. Data are expressed as means ± SD, n = 4 mice in each group. && P < 0.01 vs. Sham; ⁎⁎P˂ 0.01 vs. I/R + Saline.

GCD-PEG-QK administration ameliorated cardiac fibrosis and remodeling of myocardial I/R mice. (A) Representative images of transverse heart sections stained with Masson's trichrome at 28 days after myocardial I/R surgery. Scale bar, 50 μm. (B) Fibrosis was quantified as the ratio of fibrotic area to total area. (C) The relative mRNA level of Col1 (normalized by GAPDH) and (D) Col3 (normalized by GAPDH) in myocardial tissues. (E) Representative images of heart sections stained with WGA at 28 days after myocardial I/R surgery. Scale bar, 50 μm. The illustration shows representative image of the heart. (F) Quantitative analysis of cardiomyocyte cross-sectional area by WGA staining from 12 randomly chosen fields of four mice in each group. (G) Ratio of heart weight to body weight (HW/BW), n = 6–8. (H) Ratio of heart weight to tibial length (HW/TL), n = 6–8. (I) The relative mRNA level of Nppa (normalized by GAPDH) and (J) Nppb (normalized by GAPDH) in myocardium at 28 days after myocardial I/R. Data are expressed as means ± SD, n = 4 mice in each group except (G) and (H). && P < 0.01 vs. Sham; ⁎ P < 0.05, ⁎⁎ P ˂ 0.01 vs. I/R + Saline.

head.ce:title: VEGF mimetic peptide-conjugated nanoparticles for magnetic resonance imaging and therapy of myocardial infarction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To reduce the mortality of myocardial infarction (MI), accurate detection of the infarct and appropriate prevention against ischemia/reperfusion (I/R) induced cardiac dysfunction are highly desired. Considering that vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) receptors are overexpressed in the infarcted heart and VEGF mimetic peptide QK binds specifically to VEGF receptors and activates vascularization, the PEG-QK-modified, gadolinium-doped carbon dots (GCD-PEG-QK) were formulated. This research aims to investigate the magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) capability of GCD-PEG-QK on myocardial infarct and their therapeutic effect on I/R-induced myocardial injury. These multifunctional nanoparticles exhibited good colloidal stability, excellent fluorescent and magnetic property, and satisfactory biocompatibility. Intravenous injection of GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles post myocardial I/R displayed accurate MRI of the infarct, enhanced efficacy of QK peptide on pro-angiogenesis, and amelioration of cardiac fibrosis, remodeling and dysfunction, probably via the improvement on QK's in vivo stability and MI-targeting. Collectively, the data suggested that this theranostic nanomedicine can realize precise MRI and effective therapy for acute MI in a non-invasive manner.

Schematic diagram describing the preparation of GCD-PEG-QK and its application on magnetic resonance imaging and therapy of myocardial infarction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data included in this study are available upon request by contact with the corresponding author.

ce:section.ce:para: Cardiovascular disease, particularly, myocardial infarction (MI), has become one of the leading causes of human death [1]. Accurate measurement of tissue viability plays a pivotal role in diagnosis and treatment planning. Echocardiography can detect wall motion abnormalities but cannot provide tissue-specific information such as fibrosis. PET and SPECT can give such information, however, are with the disadvantages of ionizing radiation and low spatial resolution. Cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) imaging, which combines the evaluation of cardiac morphology, tissue viability, ventricular function, and myocardial perfusion in one investigation, has attracted more and more attention, and become the gold standard in the diagnosis of ischemic heart disease [2,3]. Traditional CMR contrast agents used for the assessment of MI are mostly gadolinium chelates, like Gd-DTPA, with non-specific distribution, low relaxivity, rapid clearance, and potential renal risks [4], therefore hindering the safe and efficient detection of MI. Hence, new contrast agents are currently under urgent research and development. It is well known that the toxicity of traditional gadolinium chelates is mostly coming from leaky Gd3+ ions. Current strategies are focused on encapsulating Gd3+ into nano-carriers to efficiently restrain Gd3+ leakage while acquiring a good longitudinal relaxivity [5]. It is noteworthy that Gd3+ encapsulated into carbon dots remains particularly stable with minimal leakage even in harsh biological environments [6]. Moreover, carbon dots, a class of fluorescent nanoparticles, possess plenty of intriguing features, like excellent water solubility, superior photostability, high safety and environmental friendliness [5].

Currently, the most effective therapy for acute MI is the restoration of blood flow through the occluded coronary artery, such as percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI). However, myocardial tissue suffers from additional injury during such a process called myocardial ischemia/reperfusion (I/R), which directly leads to cardiac dysfunction, arrhythmia, and heart failure [7,8]. Accumulating evidence have indicated that microvascular dysfunction and cell apoptosis are the main consequences of myocardial I/R injury [9,10]. Hence, stimulating angiogenesis or preventing cell apoptosis has emerged as a promising strategy for the prevention of I/R-induced heart dysfunction [11,12]. Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), a powerful and well-characterized angiogenic factor, plays an important role in cardiac vascularization. VEGF releases nitric oxide (NO) and prostacyclin (PGI2), therefore promoting vasodilation and suppressing platelet activation and neutrophils adhesion via activating the VEGF receptor (VEGFR) [13,14]. Proangiogenic therapies with VEGF-encapsulated nanoparticles or microparticles have shown profound effects in the promotion of vasculogenesis both in vitro and in vivo [15,16]. Cardiomyocytes are also the source and target of VEGF and express its receptors. VEGFR is upregulated after MI [17] and its activation exerts a direct beneficial effect on infarcted myocardium by inhibiting mitochondria permeability transition pore opening [13], therefore protecting cardiomyocytes from apoptosis and promoting cardiac contractility [17].

A few clinical trials have been performed to deliver VEGF via adenoviral plasmid [18], non-viral plasmid [19], or recombinant protein [20] to treat cardiovascular diseases. Although some of these trials showed moderate improvement in cardiac function, the success was limited because VEGF still exhibits some limitations, such as short plasma half-life (about 30 min), easy degradation by proteases, immunogenicity, and high cost of production. New techniques with accurate identification of binding sites provide the possibility to use the bioactive peptide sequences to replace the exact protein [21]. One of the most promising candidates developed from the structure of VEGF is the QK peptide (15-amino acid sequence), which mimics the 17–25 alpha region of VEGF165 [22]. QK peptide displays competitive binding to the main VEGFR, including VEGFR-1 (Flt1) and VEGFR-2 (Kdr), and can recapitulate the biological properties of VEGF by inducing the activation of VEGFR dimerization, autophosphorylation of intracellular tyrosine residues and subsequent activation of signaling pathways (such as ERK1/2), and finally leads to endothelial cells rescue from apoptosis, proliferation, and migration [23,24]. Previous studies showed that QK peptide induces vasodilatation, capillary formation and perfusion in the ischemic hindlimb, and accelerates wound healing [25]. All these studies demonstrated that QK peptide has a similar bioactivity to VEGF. However, the protective effect of QK peptide on myocardial infarction has not been explored yet, and it should be of great interest to figure out. In addition, not only displays extensive angiogenic bioactivity, QK peptide also exhibits unusual thermodynamic stability, low production cost, absence of immunogenicity, and ease of conjugation characteristics [26]. Thereby, it is better suited for clinical therapies compared with VEGF proteins.

It has been demonstrated that small nanoparticles with a diameter between 20 and 200 nm are more likely to be taken up and retained within the infarcted myocardium by extravasating leaky endothelium because vasculature becomes increasingly permeable during ischemic injury [27]. Moreover, QK peptide binds specifically to VEGFRs, which are profoundly overexpressed in infarcted myocardium at day 3–15 post-ischemia [28,29]. Hence, QK peptide-conjugated nanoparticles could be employed for molecular imaging or facilitating targeted drug delivery in the diagnosis and therapy of acute MI. Inspired by this, we came up with the idea to construct these multifunctional theranostic nanoparticles (GCD-PEG-QK) by conjugating QK peptide with gadolinium-doped carbon dots (GCD) via NHS-PEG-Mal. We investigated the targeting ability and therapeutic action of GCD-PEG-QK on ischemic myocardium to provide a safe contrast agent for precise MI imaging and a potential nanomedicine for noninvasive MI therapy.

QK peptide (KKLTWQELYQL[K(Ac)]Y[K(Ac)]GI, acetyl groups are represented as Ac, 99%) was obtained from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). TUNEL detection kit (C1098) was purchased from Beyotime (Beijing, China). Glutathione (GSH), Mal-PEG2000-NHS, GdCl3·6H2O, and Masson trichrome staining kit (HT15) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (St. Louis, USA). Matrigel® basement membrane matrix (356231) was got from Corning Inc. (San Jose, USA). The caspase-3 activity ELISA kit (C113–50) was purchased from Applygen (Beijing, China). The anti-Flt1/VEGFR1 rabbit pAb (ab2350) and anti-Kdr/VEGFR2 rabbit pAb (ab2349) were obtained from Abcam (Cambridge, UK). The Platelet and endothelial cell adhesion molecule 1 (Pecam1) rabbit pAb (GB11063–2), α-SMA rabbit pAb (GB111364), Alexa Fluor 488 labeled goat anti-rabbit IgG (GB25303), and Cy3 labeled goat anti-rabbit IgG (GB21303) were obtained from Servicebio (Wuhan, China).

GCD-SH was synthesized through a microwave-assisted pyrolysis approach. Briefly, 11.91 mg of GdCl3·6H2O, 50 mg of citric acid, and 50 mg of GSH were dissolved in 15 mL of ultrapure water under ultrasonic vibration for 5 min, and then the solution was heated in a domestic microwave oven (750 W, Galanz) for 15 min. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22-μm filter to remove large particles. Finally, the product was purified by dialysis against ultrapure water using a dialysis membrane (100 Da molecular weight cut-off) for 12 h to obtain the colorless transparent aqueous dispersion containing GCD-SH. After freeze-drying, the product was stored in a dark place at 4 °C for further applications.

Briefly, 5 mg of QK peptide and 10 mg of Mal-PEG2000-NHS were dissolved in 25 mL PBS (pH 7.4) and stirred at 25 °C for 4 h. Then 30 mg of GCD-SH was added to the PBS solution and stirred at 25 °C for another 2 h under the protection of nitrogen gas, followed by dialysis against ultrapure water using a dialysis membrane (3500 Da molecular weight cut-off) for 24 h. The final product GCD-PEG-QK was obtained by lyophilization.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of GCD-SH and GCD-PEG-QK were obtained from a JEM-2100 HRTEM (JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV voltage. Zeta potential and size distribution were examined on a Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 particle analyzer (Malvern, UK) at room temperature. UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra were performed on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo) and an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained from an IR Prestige-21 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo, USA) was applied to analyze the elemental composition. The content of Gd was assessed on an inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, Varian 710-ES, USA). The fluorescence quantum yield of the GCD-SH was determined by a comparative method using quinine sulfate as a reference [30]. To assess the content of thiol present in GCD-SH, the Ellman's assay was performed [31]. To determine the secondary structure of QK on GCD-PEG-QK, circular dichroism (CD) experiments were carried out. Briefly, QK peptide and GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles (100 μM QK) were incubated with PBS (pH 7.4) at 4 °C overnight. The concentrations of QK were determined by UV–vis spectrophotometry at 280 nm. Then, the CD spectra were obtained on a JASCO J-810 spectrometer (Easton, MD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and in vitro fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: H9c2 cells and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) were purchased from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, USA). The HUVECs were cultured in DMEM (Gibco, USA) supplemented with 0.04 mg/mL endothelial cell growth supplement (ECGS, Gibco, USA), 10% FBS (Corning, Australia) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin (Gibco, USA) in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C. Cells from passages 4–8 were used for all the experiments. H9c2 cells were cultured in high glucose DMEM (4500 mg/L glucose) supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin/streptomycin in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C.

To visualize the cellular uptake of nanoparticles by H9c2 cardiomyoblast-like cells and HUVECs under hypoxic and normoxic conditions, fluorescence imaging was performed. Cells were first treated with CoCl2 (400 μM) for 24 h to mimic hypoxic conditions (not real hypoxia), followed by administration of the nanoparticles for 4 h, and then washed with PBS. Nuclear staining was conducted by incubating with NucBeacon™ Green Nucleic Acid Stain (IGeneBio, Guangzhou, China) for 15 min. A Leica TCS SP5 confocal laser scanning microscope (Wetzlar, Germany) was employed to visualize the fluorescence images.

Relaxivity measurements were performed on a 7.0 T MR scanner (Bruker Pharmascan, Germany) with the RARE-T1 + T2-map sequence as follows: TR/TE = 500/9 ms (T1), TR/TE = 3000/45 ms (T2), FA = 180°, slice thickness = 1 mm, and FOV = 40 × 40 mm2 [28]. The relaxivity value of r
1 (r
2 ) was calculated from the slope of the linear plot of 1/T1 (1/T2) versus the Gd concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Animal model and in vivo MRI
ce:section.ce:para: C57BL/6 J mice (male, 8 weeks) were obtained from Capital Medical University. All animal procedures were approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of Capital Medical University (AEEI-2020-116) and following the National Institutes of Health guide for the care and use of Laboratory animals (NIH Publications No. 8023, revised 1978). Anesthesia of mice was conducted with 2% isoflurane. The pericardium was opened and the left anterior descending artery (LAD) was occluded for 30 min, then reperfusion was allowed by ligation release. Blanching of the myocardium provided direct evidence of ligation. The same procedure was performed on the sham group except for the ligation of LAD.

Five days after I/R surgery, CMR imaging was performed before and up to 24 h after administration of GCD or GCD-PEG-QK (20 μmol Gd/kg) with a 7.0 T small animal MR scanner (Bruker Pharmascan, Germany) under ECG triggering and respiratory gating (SA Instruments, Stony Brook). A fast gradient echo FLASH measurement was performed, with the following parameters [30]: 16 frames per heart cycle (TR = 7.0–12.0 ms); TE = 2.7 ms; flip angle = 60°; slice thickness = 1 mm. Contrast to noise ratio (CNR) between infarcted and non-infarcted area was calculated as CNR = (μi-μn)/σ noise (μi or μn: mean signal intensity of infarcted or non-infarcted area; σ noise: standard deviation of the noise).

4, 24, or 48 h post i.v. injection of the nanoparticles (20 μmol Gd/kg) via the tail vein, mice were euthanized. The major organs including the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys were harvested and the ex vivo imaging of the organs was captured using the In vivo Imaging System (PerkinElmer, MA, USA) with an excitation filter of 430 nm and an emission filter of 500 nm. The Gd level in the infarcted myocardium, non-infarcted myocardium, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney at 24 h post i.v. injection of the nanoparticles (20 μmol Gd/kg) was measured. Briefly, the major organs were cut into small pieces and predigested with 3 mL of nitric acid overnight. Then, 1 mL of 30% H2O2 was added. After microwave-assisted digestion (MARS5, CEM), the Gd level was measured by inductively coupled plasma-Mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, Agilent 8800, USA).

For validation of the infarct-targeting capability, 4 and 24 h after i.v. injection of GCD-PEG or GCD-PEG-QK, heart tissues were harvested. After embedding and freezing in OCT, the left ventricle was cryosectioned into 5-μm thick sections. Immunofluorescence staining was then performed to identify the Flt1 and Kdr in the infarct and non-infarct zone of the left ventricle by using Flt1 (1:200) or Kdr (1:200) first antibody. Then the secondary antibody Alexa Fluor 488 labeled goat anti-rabbit IgG or Cy3 labeled goat anti-rabbit IgG (antibody information refer to 2.1) was incubated respectively. Negative controls without the incubation of the first antibody were carried out. The fluorescence images were recorded by a Leica SP5 confocal laser scanning microscope.

To evaluate angiogenesis, a wound healing assay and a Matrigel tube-formation assay were carried out [32]. The wound healing assay was performed to evaluate the migration of HUVECs. Briefly, HUVECs were seeded in six-well plates. In each well, the 100% confluent monolayers of cells were scratched with a sterile pipette tip to generate a cell-free zone and washed three times with PBS to remove debris. Then the HUVECs were treated with PBS, GCD-PEG, QK (10 μM), or GCD-PEG-QK (10 μM QK) in DMEM medium (without ECGS) at 37 °C. Photos were taken by a microscope at 0, 12, and 24 h after the administration, respectively. Six images per treatment group at each time point were analyzed.

For the Matrigel tube-formation assay, the HUVECs were first treated with CoCl2 (400 μM) to mimic the hypoxic condition (not real hypoxia), and then washed with PBS. Next, the cells in DMEM medium (without ECGS) with PBS, GCD-PEG, QK (10 μM), or GCD-PEG-QK (10 μM QK) were seeded in 96-well plates precoated with Matrigel at a density of 2 × 104 cells/well. Tube formation was observed at 4 h after seeding. A total of six images per treatment group were taken. The number of branches per magnified field was quantified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GCD-PEG-QK on cardiac function, angiogenesis, cardiac remodeling, and fibrosis in I/R mice
ce:section.ce:para: Mice were divided into four groups, and treated with intravenous injection of normal saline, QK (2.5 mg QK/kg), or GCD-PEG-QK (2.5 mg QK/kg, equals 1 μmol Gd/kg) at day-3 and -5 post I/R surgery. Left ventricle function was evaluated under anesthesia with inhalation of 1.5% isoflurane at day-1, −14, and − 28 after I/R surgery by M-mode echocardiography (Vevo 2100 imaging system, VisualSonics, Toronto; 6–8 mice in each group).

14 days after I/R surgery, some mice were sacrificed. Hearts were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, embedded in paraffin, and cut into 5-μm-thick sections. The sections were incubated by anti-α-SMA (1:200) or Pecam1 (1:200) first antibody at 4 °C overnight, and then stained with Alexa Fluor 488 or Cy-3 labeled secondary antibody at room temperature for 1 h. Total α-SMA+ round vessels, Pecam1+ cells, and TUNEL+ cells in the border zone of the infarcted heart or normal heart were counted in 12 randomly chosen fields from four different mice in each group. The Image J software was used to analyze α-SMA+ round vessels and Pecam1+ cells with vessel J (plugin). The Image Pro Plus 6.0 was used to analyze the TUNEL data. Proteins were harvested for detection of caspase-3 activity by an ELISA kit (4 mice in each group).

28 days after I/R surgery, the left mice were sacrificed. Heart weight, tibial length, and body weight were measured (6–8 mice in each group). Hearts were embedded in paraffin, cut into 5-μm thick sections, incubated with wheat germ agglutinin (WGA) to detect cardiomyocyte hypertrophy, and incubated with Masson's Trichrome to detect fibrosis (4 mice in each group). The Image J software was used to analyze the data.

Pecam1, natriuretic peptide A (Nppa), natriuretic peptide B (Nppb), collagen type I (Col1), collagen type III (Col3), Flt1, Kdr, and GAPDH mRNA levels in the heart were determined by real time-PCR (4 mice in each group). Briefly, total RNA was extracted from myocardial tissues using TRIzol reagent and reverse-transcribed into cDNA with the ReverTra Ace qPCR RT Kit (TOYOBO). Then, equal amounts of cDNA products were subjected to PCR amplification with SYBR Green Realtime PCR Master Mix (TOYOBO) and primers on a 7500 Real-Time PCR System (Applied Biosystems). Primers for Pecam1, Nppa, Nppb, Col1, Col3, Flt1, Kdr, and GAPDH were summarized in Table S1. Each sample was normalized by GAPDH.

14 days after two successive intravenous injections of GCD-PEG-QK (20 μmol Gd/kg), normal saline, or one intravenous injection of Gd-DTPA to the normal mice, major organs were harvested for histological analysis with hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining (6 mice in each group). Blood serum was collected to detect the levels of alanine transaminase (ALT), aspartate transaminase (AST), and creatinine (CREA) by commercial ELISA kits. Blood routine tests were also carried out (6 mice in each group). For hemolysis analysis, red blood cells separated from three mice in each group were seeded into 96-well plates. GCD-PEG-QK, PBS (negative control), or H2O (positive control) was added to the wells and co-cultured for 2 h at 37 °C. After centrifugation, the supernatants of the samples were measured at 570 nm by a multifunctional microplate reader.

Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation of at least three independent measurements, unless indicated differently. One-way analysis of variance followed by post hoc least significant difference pairwise multiple comparison procedure was used. P < 0.05 was considered statistical significance.

TEM images showed that GCD-SH presents spherical morphology with a narrow size distribution (Fig. 1A). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) illustrated the average diameter of GCD-SH is around 4.32 ± 0.67 nm (Fig. 1B). GCD-SH in deionized water, PBS (pH 7.4), and cell medium (with 10% FBS) showed excellent aqueous dispersibility, maintaining good colloidal stability at room temperature without visible precipitation for at least one month (Fig. 1A). The zeta potentials of GCD-SH in water, PBS, and cell medium were − 12.80, −10.80, and − 8.43 mV, respectively on day-1, and were observed until day-14, during which no significant change was found (Fig. 1C).

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission of GCD-SH were observed to illustrate the optical properties. The characteristic absorption of carbon dots is at 350 nm; an n − π* transition peak appeared at 240 nm corresponding to the strong interaction between the carbon dots and Gd3+. The photograph (inset) of the dispersion under a UV lamp displays a bright blue colour (Fig. 1D). The GCD-SH exhibits a maximum fluorescence emission at 420 nm with the excitation at 350 nm (Fig. 1E). In addition, changing pH from 3 to 9 did not significantly affect the intensity of fluorescence, until reach pH = 10, the intensity value was markedly reduced (Fig. 1F and Fig. S1B). The fluorescent quantum yield of GCD-SH was measured to be 32% by comparison with quinine sulfate (54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference (Fig. S1A).

The results of XPS exhibited the contents of elements and valence states of elements in GCD-SH. Fig. S2A showed the typical sharp peaks of C 1 s (285 eV), N 1 s (398 eV), O 1 s (530 eV), S 2p (162 eV), and Gd 4d (148 eV). The relative atomic percentages of C, N, O, S, and Gd in GCD-SH were 60.94%, 5.64%, 31.05%, 1.22%, and 1.16%, respectively. The C 1 s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. S2B) showed three peaks after fitting, i.e., CC (285.07 eV), CS (286.03 eV), and C=O/C-O (288.8 eV). The N 1 s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. S2D) showed two peaks after fitting, i.e., CN (399.90 eV) and C-N-H (400.58 eV). The peaks at 531.5 eV and 532.2 eV in the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2E) correspond to CO and CO respectively. The S 2p high-resolution spectrum (Fig. S2F) can be fitted into three peaks, i.e., -C-SOx- (165.17 eV), -C-S-C- (164.40 eV) and GdS (163.83 eV). The Gd 4d high-resolution peaks centered at Gd 4d5/2 (143.84 eV) and Gd 4d3/2 (149.2 eV) indicated the presence of Gd3+ in the GCD-SH (Fig. S2C). The content of -SH in GCD-SH was measured to be around 1.91% by Ellman's assay. The above results illustrated that the GCD-SH surface is equipped with -SH, -COOH, -OH, and -NH2 as functional groups, and that is consistent with our initial design.

QK peptide was conjugated to GCD-SH via Mal-PEG2000-NHS, leading to the formation of GCD-PEG-QK. First, the dimensional and morphological properties of the obtained GCD-PEG-QK were characterized by TEM. As compared with GCD-SH, the GCD-PEG-QK revealed a spherical morphology with a typical core-shell structure (Fig. 2A) and an increased size of 103 ± 11.88 nm according to the DLS data (Fig. 2B). GCD-PEG-QK in deionized water, PBS (pH 7.4), and cell medium (with 10% FBS) also showed excellent aqueous dispersibility, maintaining good colloidal stability at room temperature without visible precipitation for at least one month (Fig. 2A).

Zeta potential, UV–vis absorption, fluorescence emission, and CD spectra were analyzed to confirm the successful construction of GCD-PEG-QK. After conjugation with PEG or PEG-QK, the zeta potential of GCD-SH changed from −9.92 ± 0.11 mV to −11.63 ± 0.42 mV or − 5.39 ± 0.86 mV, respectively. UV–vis spectra indicated the peaks of GCD-PEG-QK at 280 and 350 nm corresponding to the characteristic absorption of tryptophan on QK peptide and absorption of GCD-SH, respectively (Fig. 2D). Compared with GCD-SH, the fluorescence intensities of GCD-PEG and GCD-PEG-QK were partly quenched but without wavelength shift after conjugation (data not shown). The FTIR of GCD-PEG-QK showed the peaks at 3292 cm−1 (NH), 1516 cm−1 (NH), and 1652 cm−1 (νC=O) corresponding to the characteristic absorption of QK peptide [33], and the peaks at 2872 cm−1 (νCH2), 1455 cm−1 (δCH2) and 1106 cm−1 (νC-O-C) attributed to the characteristic absorption of PEG (Fig. 2E and F). CD spectra were employed to investigate the structural characteristics of QK and GCD-PEG-QK (Fig. 2C). As expected, QK and GCD-PEG-QK exhibited the characteristic CD spectra of alpha helices, showing double minima at around 208 and 222 nm [23]. The above results suggested that the QK peptide and PEG were successfully conjugated with GCD-SH.

It has been reported that VEGFRs are notably increased in ischemic tissue and hypoxic cells [17,29]. Our results also confirmed that both VEGFR-1 (Flt1) and VEGFR-2 (Kdr) were significantly upregulated in I/R hearts (Fig. S3, P < 0.01). Therefore, the in vitro targeting capability of GCD-PEG-QK was examined by visualizing the cellular uptake of nanoparticles by H9c2 cardiomyoblast-like cells and HUVECs under hypoxic or normoxic conditions. Here, CoCl2 was used to mimic hypoxia conditions (not true hypoxia) [34]. After 4 h incubation with GCD-PEG or GCD-PEG-QK, cells were washed to remove the unbinding extracellular nanoparticles. GCD-PEG treated group merely showed slight blue emission, whereas GCD-PEG-QK treated cells emitted bright blue fluorescence. More importantly, the fluorescence intensity of GCD-PEG-QK was significantly higher in CoCl2 pretreated cells compared with normoxic cells (Fig. S4, P < 0.01), suggesting higher uptake under hypoxic conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo MRI
ce:section.ce:para:
In vitro relaxivity studies of GCD-SH were performed on a 7.0 T MRI scanner system, and the r
1 and r
2 values were calculated according to Gd concentrations (Fig. S1C and D). Aqueous suspensions with a concentration of Gd varying between 0 and 0.4 mM were scanned. The results showed an r
1 equal to 12.76 mM−1 s−1, which is much higher than that of the commercially available contrast agents, such as Gd-DTPA (3.57 mM−1 s−1 [35]). The r
2
/r
1 ratio is a key factor to determine whether a contrast agent has the potential to be a T1 or a T2 contrast agent. Theoretically, the r
2
/r
1 ratio close to 1 unit is desirable for a T1 contrast agent. And the ratio of GCD-SH is 1.22, suggesting that it has excellent potential as a T1 contrast agent.

Encouraged by its high r
1 value, in vivo MR imaging contrast capability for MI was then evaluated. Mice were injected with GCD-SH or GCD-PEG-QK (20 μmol Gd/kg) via the tail vein. Before injection, the left ventricles exhibited no signal enhancement. And 5 min after the injection, a notable signal enhancement (yellow arrow) could be seen in the infarcted area. The contrast for MRI was quantified by assessing the contrast-to-noise ratio (CNR) between infarcted and remote myocardium within 24 h post-injection. Data showed that GCD-SH injection generated a rapid increase in CNR, which reached the highest point at 30 min and disappeared at 24 h (Fig. 3A and C). On the other hand, GCD-PEG-QK injection exhibited prolonged retention and significantly higher level of CNR compared with GCD-SH from 1 to 24 h after administration (Fig. 3B and C, P < 0.01), suggesting a receptor-mediated targeting to the infarcted myocardium besides the EPR effect. Therefore, GCD-PEG-QK could be used as an efficient MRI contrast agent for the detection of acute MI.

To determine the capability of GCD-PEG-QK remaining in the infarcted myocardium, the fluorescence of nanoparticles in the left ventricles of I/R mice was observed after i.v. injection. As shown in Fig. 4 , both Flt1 and Kdr positive staining was observed predominantly surrounding the necrotic areas, which is consistent with the previous reports [17,29]. There was a much more amount of GCD-PEG-QK found in the infarcted myocardium compared with the remote area of I/R heart at both 4 and 24 h (Fig. 4A). Whereas there was less GCD-PEG retained in the infarcted or remote area at 4 or 24 h (Fig. 4B). The fluorescence of GCD-PEG-QK was weak in the normal heart compared with I/R heart at 4 and 24 h (Fig. S5A). Consistently, the ICP data showed that 24 h post i.v. injection (20 μmol Gd/kg), the GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles retained in the infarcted myocardium were about 7.9 μg/g (Gd/tissue), which were much higher than those in the non-infarcted myocardium (1.2 μg/g). By contrast, the GCD-PEG nanoparticles were 1.3 μg/g in the infarcted myocardium vs. 0.9 μg/g in the non-infarcted myocardium (Fig. S5B). Together, the above data indicated that with the conjugation of QK peptide, the GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles showed much more retaining in the infarct where Flt1 and Kdr are overexpressed, suggesting a ligand-receptor mediated interaction besides the EPR phenomenon.

To determine the biodistribution of GCD-PEG-QK in major organs of I/R mice, fluorescence imaging (Fig. S5) at different time points post i.v. injection was explored. The data indicated that GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles mainly accumulated in the heart, liver, lungs, and kidneys of I/R mice at 4 and 24 h. Consistently, the ICP data indicated that the Gd content in the liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys were about 1.0, 0.1, 0.7 and 0.8 μg/g at 24 h post GCD-PEG-QK injection (Fig. S5C). After 48 h, the fluorescence imaging showed that all the nanoparticles disappeared from the major organs (Fig. S5A), suggesting the GCD-PEG-QK can be excreted out of the body quickly. This is important, because the nanoparticles do not show undesirable accumulation in major organs, therefore causing less in vivo toxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro protective effect of GCD-PEG-QK
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize the capacity of GCD-PEG-QK for pro-angiogenesis, we investigated its effect on HUVECs migration and tube formation. Previous studies have demonstrated that QK peptide promotes endothelial tube formation [36], thus we used QK as a positive control. The results showed that after 12 and 24 h of administration, both QK and GCD-PEG-QK markedly promoted HUVECs migration (P < 0.01) (Fig. 5A and C). Similarly, both QK and GCD-PEG-QK administration greatly improved the number of branches under the H/R condition (Fig. 5B and D, P < 0.01). While the GCD-PEG group did not show any difference compared with the control group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo protective effect and mechanism of GCD-PEG-QK against myocardial I/R injury
ce:section.ce:para: After myocardial I/R surgery, QK, GCD-PEG-QK, or saline was injected at day-3 and -5 via the tail vein in different groups of mice (Fig. 6A, B). Heart function was examined by echocardiography at 1, 14, and 28 days after I/R surgery (Fig. 6C). Results showed that there was no difference in left-ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) and left-ventricular anterior wall (LVAW) between each group at day-1 (Fig. 6C–F). However, LVEF (P < 0.01) and systolic LVAW (P < 0.01) of I/R mice were significantly reduced at day-14 and day-28 compared with the sham group. Both QK and GCD-PEG-QK administration notably ameliorated LVEF (P < 0.05) and systolic LVAW (QK, P < 0.05; GCD-PEG-QK, P < 0.01) at day-14 (Fig. 6D–F) compared with I/R group; while only GCD-PEG-QK had a significant effect on LVEF (P < 0.05), diastolic LVAW (P < 0.05) and systolic LVAW (P < 0.01) at day-28 post I/R surgery compared with I/R group (Fig. 6D–F). In addition, the changes in LVEF from day-1 to day-14 (Δ LVEF 14–1 day) and from day-1 to day-28 (Δ LVEF 28–1 day) were calculated. The data showed that I/R induced marked LVEF reduction at both day-14 and day-28 (P < 0.01), and only GCD-PEG-QK significantly ameliorated it compared with the I/R group (Δ14–1 day, P < 0.05; Δ28–1 day, P < 0.01, Fig. 6G).

To figure out the mechanism of GCD-PEG-QK on the protective effect against I/R- induced myocardial injury, we investigated whether GCD-PEG-QK was sufficient to stimulate vessel formation in vivo. The α-SMA+ round vessels and Pecam1+ cells around the infarcted area were quantified and found to be significantly (P < 0.01) enhanced with the administration of GCD-PEG-QK at 14 days post-I/R (Fig. 7A–D). Consistently, the mRNA level of Pecam1 around the infarct was also found to be markedly upregulated with GCD-PEG-QK treatment (Fig. 7E, P < 0.01). However, QK peptide alone did not induce Pecam1 or α-SMA expression. These results demonstrated that GCD-PEG-QK but not QK could effectively promote angiogenesis around the infarct.

Furthermore, the protection effect of GCD-PEG-QK against I/R-induced cell apoptosis was examined by TUNEL staining and caspase-3 activity measurement at 14 days post I/R surgery. A noteworthy increase in the number of TUNEL-positive cells and caspase-3 activity was found in the I/R group compared with the sham group (P < 0.01). Both QK and GCD-PEG-QK administration exhibited a prominent anti-apoptotic effect, which was evidenced by TUNEL-positive staining (Fig. 7F and G, P < 0.01) and caspase-3 activity (Fig. 7H, P < 0.01).

To evaluate the protective effect of QK and GCD-PEG-QK on cardiac fibrosis, Masson's trichrome staining was employed to identify collagens (Fig. 8A). The GCD-PEG-QK group exhibited notably lower collagen deposition compared with the I/R + Saline group (P < 0.05), whereas the QK group exhibited no significant change (Fig. 8B). RT-PCR results showed that I/R injury notably upregulated mRNA levels of Col1 (P < 0.01) and Col3 (P < 0.01), and those were attenuated both by QK (P < 0.05) and GCD-PEG-QK (P < 0.01) (Fig. 8C and D). Together, the above data indicated that both QK and GCD-PEG-QK attenuated cardiac fibrosis and the benefit from GCD-PEG-QK was more remarkable.

Regarding cardiac remodeling, heart weight/ body weight (HW/BW), heart weight/ tibial length (HW/TL), WGA-staining, and mRNA levels of two hypertrophy-related markers (Nppa and Nppb) were observed. All the results indicated that GCD-PEG-QK, but not QK (except Nppa/GAPDH, P < 0.01), markedly attenuated I/R elicited cardiac remodeling at 28 days post I/R injury (Fig. 8E–J, P < 0.01).

The above data indicated that the GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles were efficient in targeting infarcts and providing therapy for ischemic heart disease. However, toxic injury, which might be caused by GCD-PEG-QK presents a concern. It is well known that the toxicity of Gd-derived contrast agents is mostly brought about by the leakage of free Gd3+. Therefore, Gd3+ ion leakage from GCD-PEG-QK was first tested in PBS (pH 7.4) at 37 °C. Results showed almost no free Gd3+ could be detected within one week of dialysis (data not shown), indicating the GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles are stable enough to be used as a probe in the human body.

Further, considering the nanoparticles were administered systemically, their hemolytic potential was assessed. Data showed that the hemolysis rates at different concentrations of the nanoparticles were much lower than the hemolysis limit (5%) (Fig. S6A), suggesting the hemocompatibility is quite well, which makes GCD-PEG-QK suitable for intravenous application. Next, the pathological changes of the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, kidneys, and brain were investigated by H&E staining at 14 days after two successive i.v. injections of GCD-PEG-QK (20 μmol Gd/kg). Results showed no obvious abnormalities or inflammatory responses and were comparable to the control group (Fig. S6B). On the other hand, 14 days after one i.v. injection of Gd-DTPA at its commonly used MRI dose, liver, and kidney exhibited observable inflammatory cell infiltration (Fig. S6B) as previously reported [37]. Moreover, biochemical parameters including ALT, AST, and CREA, and hematology parameters were taken. There was no difference between the control and nanoparticles group in these indicators (Table S2), suggesting good biocompatibility of GCD-PEG-QK and demonstrating their potential for clinical diagnosis and therapy.

In vitro study provided evidence that the GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles maintained the ability to induce angiogenesis (Fig. 5). Furthermore, in vivo study suggested that the protective effects of QK peptide against I/R induced myocardial injury were markedly enhanced by GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles (Fig. 6–8), probably via improved in vivo stability and infarct-targeting capability. Intravenous injection of GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles post-MI effectively improved the reparative potency of QK on pro-angiogenesis (Fig. 7), therefore preventing cardiac fibrosis and remodeling (Fig. 8) and improving heart function (Fig. 6). In addition, this is the first study applying QK peptide-conjugated nanoparticles as a cardiac MRI contrast agent to detect infarct, which displayed longer detection window, higher relaxivity, and lower toxicity compared with the commercially used Gd-DTPA [37].

Nowadays, most of the available regenerative therapies are suited to patients in chronic stages of MI as they require echo-guided intramyocardial injection or catheter-based transendocardial delivery of medicine, which are prone to cause heart-wall rupture or arrhythmias during the acute stage of MI [38,39]. Ideally, systemic injection would be much less invasive, whereas it remains a major challenge in MI treatment due to less controlled drug biodistribution, short circulation time, and off-target effects. To overcome these problems, bioengineering-based platforms have been built to specifically deliver drugs to the target of interest and reduce off-target effects. Several studies have proven the possibility of using i.v. injection for efficacious delivery of medicine to the infarct. For example, Nguyen et al. [40] described a method for delivering MMP-cleavable peptide conjugated nanoparticles to the site of infarct on an MI rat model via i.v. injection. Scott et al. [38,41] have successfully delivered P-selectin antibody conjugated nanoparticles to P-selectin-overexpressed vasculature of infarct by i.v. injection. The current study realized a successful delivery of GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles to the infarcted regions via i.v. injection. More importantly, it did provide us satisfactory therapeutic effect on I/R-induced heart injury.

Free QK peptide is easily degraded by proteases and washed away from the myocardium, whereas PEGylation masks the peptide's surface without affecting its binding site and increases the molecular size, therefore preventing degradation by proteolytic enzymes and decreasing its renal filtration. When PEG is properly conjugated to a peptide, it modifies many of its characteristics but normally maintains its receptor recognition and bioactivity [42]. As previously indicated, the size of nanoparticles partially controls their ability to pass through biological barriers, therefore their biodistributions and retentions [43]. During I/R, the vasculature of the ischemic heart becomes increasingly permeable, which provides passive targeting to infarct via the enhanced permeation and retention (EPR) effect [38]. In our study, the GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles were around 100 nm, allowing for enhanced myocardial retention in the infarcted areas as previously indicated [27]. In addition, at 3–15 days post myocardial ischemia, expressions of VEGFR are markedly increased in the infarcted heart [28,29]. Our results also showed that the mRNA level (Fig. S3) and fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4) of Flt1 and Kdr were much higher in the infarct, hence enhancing the targeting capability of GCD-PEG-QK to the infarct via a ligand-receptor (QK-VEGFR) interaction.

QK peptide, which binds to the main VEGFR, has been widely studied and applied as a promising candidate for the promotion of angiogenesis in tissue regeneration and repair [21,26,44]. QK shows several advantages over the various VEGF family members, including superior stability, lower preparation costs, slim chance of denaturalization, and absence of immunogenicity [45,46]. The in vitro study exhibited a similar capability to induce capillary formation by QK peptide and GCD-PEG-QK (Fig. 5), while the in vivo study indicated higher effectiveness of GCD-PEG-QK in promoting angiogenesis and ameliorating I/R induced cardiac dysfunction, fibrosis, and remodeling (Fig. 6–8), possibly due to enhanced in vivo stability, reduced off-target effect and prolonged retention in the infarct compared to systemic QK administration. Besides the direct modulation of angiogenesis, the protection of cardiomyocytes from apoptosis is another mechanism of QK treatment. Numerous studies have indicated that VEGFR activation exerts a powerful anti-apoptotic effect on cardiomyocytes, endothelial cells, neurons, and skeletal muscle cells [17]. Consistently, our results demonstrated that both QK and GCD-PEG-QK profoundly inhibited I/R-induced cell apoptosis (Fig. 7F–H).

As demonstrated in Fig. S5, the GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles were predominantly accumulated in the infarcted heart within 24 h and cleared away after 48 h. Almost no fluorescence could be seen in the major organs at 48 h post-injection, suggesting no undesirable accumulation in the major organs and less toxicity. It is well known that MR imaging is contraindicated in patients with severe renal diseases because of the risk of nephrogenic systemic fibrosis caused by Gd3+ ions released from Gd-based contrast agents, like Gd-DTPA [47]. ICP study demonstrated the advantages of GCD-PEG-QK nanoparticles in preventing Gd3+ ion leaking over the traditional Gd-chelates, indicating higher chemical stability and lower toxicity. As demonstrated in the animal study, GCD-PEG-QK administration (20 times of therapeutic dose) did not cause any pathological change in major organs. However, Gd-DTPA could induce obvious liver and kidney inflammation at its commonly used imaging dose (Fig. S6B). Other toxicity studies including blood routine, blood biochemistry, and hemolysis test also exhibited good biocompatibility of GCD-PEG-QK (Fig. S6A and Table S2).

With the breakthroughs in peptide chemistry and drug delivery technologies, nowadays, peptide drugs account for a significant proportion of the pharmaceutical market benefiting from their highly selective and relatively safe characteristics. Up to 2022, approximately 80 therapeutic peptides have reached the global market [48]. Moreover, current peptide technologies provide new opportunities and directions for the future, which primarily include multifunctional and targeting peptide drugs. In the current study, the GCDs were prepared by a one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis method. Then, they were modified with PEG-QK at room temperature, which is easy and green and allows reliable production on large scales. Regarding clinical practice, day-3 and -5 post-reperfusion could be the appropriate time point for i.v. injection of the nanoparticles considering the promising results exhibited in this study. While there are also some limitations in this study. For example, prolonged activation of VEGFR may somehow increase vessel permeability or leakiness, leading to fibrin deposition, tissue swelling, and inflammation [49]. Therefore, a long-term toxicity test probably should be carried out in the future to investigate each of these possibilities.

GCD-PEG-QK, an infarct-targeted molecular probe, and nanomedicine, provides a noninvasive (i.v.) and effective MR imaging and therapeutic approach for acute MI. By targeting the overexpressed VEGFR on the infarct during acute MI, it allows safe and accurate detection of the infarcted regions. By delivering functional QK peptide to the infarcted myocardium, it significantly promotes blood vessel formation, reduces cardiac apoptosis, remodeling, and fibrosis, and finally improves cardiac function in the myocardial I/R mice model.
